text:
This study analyses the education of students with special educational needs, a theme that is widely presented in the human rights and educational reform literature. Based on an extensive literature review, it examines barriers and stimuli for inclusion in general, and the specific obstacles and additional complexities presented in Latin America. In the first instance, it aims to isolate and identify the barriers to and stimuli for inclusion, thereby describing their complex structure, based on variables such as the type of financial strategy used, the state of school premises, teacher training, and the level of articulation between schools and educational systems.

This study relies on a specific methodology that is concerned with the selection of empirical research studies, theories and policies of International Organisations (IOs) in order to achieve a critical review of the present-day mechanisms of inclusivity. According to the conclusions of this discussion, the barriers to educational inclusivity such as the general underfunding of education, teacher training and insufficient infrastructure continue to prevail and in turn are having the impact of depriving learners of quality education and emotional well-being. Based on these arguments, the general consensus is that inclusion is considered a universal advantage for all sectors of the education system, transcending its legal requirements. Finally, this research calls for an alignment of the local level of education policy with advances in scientific knowledge, in order to achieve a transformation from the conceptualization of equitable access as a remote ideal to real inclusivity in schools.
